channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Fish and Wildlife Service Clears a Weedkiller, Saying It Won’t Cause Extinction
item.description: The finding effectively paves the way for continued use of atrazine, a widely used herbicide that has been linked to birth defects and cancer in humans.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Julie Ingwersen/Reuters
item.media:description: Test plots at a Syngenta research site in Junction City, Kan. Atrazine is made primarily by Syngenta.

item.title: E.P.A. to Repeal Some Limits on ‘Forever Chemicals’ in Drinking Water
item.description: The rules were established by the Biden administration after research linked the compounds to a range of serious health problems.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, has argued that the agency made a procedural error when it established the drinking water standards in 2024.

item.title: Scientists Tweaked the Global Warming Outlook. So Trump Weighed In.
item.description: Renewable energy has helped make the worst-case scenario a bit less bad. The president said, falsely, it shows that climate scientists were wrong all along.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Solar panels and wind turbines in north-central China. Renewable is becoming more affordable in many countries.

item.title: The Quest for Clean Hydrogen Moves Underground
item.description: The dream of clean hydrogen has tantalized energy experts for years, but producing it has been tough. Many start-ups think the answer could lie beneath our feet.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Ian Willms

item.title: Drilling Into the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica
item.description: Ten people. Eight weeks. Three thousand feet to pierce a fast-melting Antarctic glacier.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong and Chang W. Lee
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times
item.media:description: Drilling equipment on the Thwaites Glacier in Antarctica in January.

item.title: The decline of the Thwaites Glacier could affect tens of millions worldwide.
item.description: A collapse would sharply accelerate sea level rise in coastal cities.
item.dc:creator: Mira Rojanasakul

item.title: Europe’s Green-Energy Future Has a Reindeer Problem
item.description: In northern Norway, Sami people fear a copper mine will disrupt their traditional lifestyles.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil, Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Michal Siarek
item.media:credit: Michał Siarek for The New York Times

item.title: The New ‘Gold Rush’ of Geothermal Energy
item.description: A new wave of start-ups are trying to harvest emissions-free energy from inside the Earth, but the industry still faces significant challenges.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Eduardo Munoz/Reuters
item.media:description: Executives of Fervo and others celebrated the geothermal company’s I.P.O. on the Nasdaq on May 13.

item.title: Manager at Trump’s N.J. Golf Club Helped Plan Reflecting Pool Repairs
item.description: David Schutzenhofer, a Trump Organization employee, reached out to one of the vendors the government later hired for the iconic site near the Lincoln Memorial, documents show.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool has been plagued for decades by leaks and algae blooms, which various administrations have been unable to permanently fix.

item.title: Rising Energy Costs and Data Centers at Heart of NextEra’s Dominion Bid
item.description: NextEra’s proposed acquisition of Dominion Energy comes as Americans are paying a lot more for electricity, and data centers are demanding a lot more power from utilities.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.media:credit: Tristan Spinski for The New York Times
item.media:description: NextEra Energy’s acquisition of Dominion Energy would consolidate utility operations along much of the East Coast, which could shape electricity service for tens of millions of customers.

item.title: Tesla’s Semi Truck could Jolt the Trucking Industry
item.description: California truckers have expressed strong interest in the Tesla Semi because it costs much less and can travel further on a charge than electric trucks sold by established manufacturers.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing and Gabriela Bhaskar
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Screens on either side of a Tesla Semi’s steering wheel provide a view of the traffic around the vehicle.

item.title: Bipartisan Bill Would Impose New Annual Fee on Electric Vehicles
item.description: A House transportation bill introduced this week would require owners of electric cars to pay $130 to cover the cost of road repairs.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times
item.media:description: Congress is considering imposing a new annual fee on electric vehicles to help pay for road repairs.

item.title: She Was Finding Sources of Dangerous Water and Soil Pollution
item.description: Melanie Malone led a research project to identify and study contamination sites in Washington State. Then the E.P.A. canceled her grant.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Meron Tekie Menghistab for The New York Times

item.title: Ten people. Eight weeks. Three thousand feet through the ice.
item.description: Here’s the story of the expedition’s most ambitious project.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times

item.title: When Tornado Weather Hits, These Scientists Break Out the Colored Pencils
item.description: With a battery of modern technology at their fingertips, meteorologists often turn first to an old-fashioned tracking technique.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones and Desiree Rios

item.title: Which Countries Are Profiting From the Iran War Oil Shock
item.description: An analysis of oil export data offers clues about which nations have benefited from higher prices, and which have lost a lot of revenue.
item.dc:creator: Amy Fan and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: How a ‘Model’ for Climate Migration Became a Cautionary Tale
item.description: The residents of Isle de Jean Charles in Louisiana found safety after moving to higher ground. But the experience left some of them warning others facing relocation: ‘Don’t do it.’
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance and Wayan Barre
item.media:credit: Wayan Barre for The New York Times

item.title: Michigan Battles Trump Over His Order to Keep an Old Coal Plant Running
item.description: The Trump administration broke the law, Michigan and others told a court, by declaring an “energy emergency” and forcing an aging coal-burning plant to keep operating.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Jim West/UCG/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The J.H. Campbell coal-burning power plant in West Olive, Mich. The state is challenging a Trump order to keep the aging plant active.

item.title: ‘Been Here Stay Here’ Documentary Examines Tangier Island
item.description: “Been Here Stay Here” examines the complicated Chesapeake Bay dynamic when many residents don’t believe in man-made climate change.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: Is Musk Running an Illegal Power Plant? The D.O.J. Says It Might Weigh In.
item.description: In a court filing, the administration signaled support for Elon Musk’s artificial intelligence company in a lawsuit challenging xAI’s generators at a huge Mississippi data center.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: The entrance to xAI’s power plant in Southaven, Miss., in February.

item.title: Here’s What It Costs to Keep a Coal Plant Open
item.description: A regulatory filing explains just how much it costs to keep an aging coal plant open under the Trump administration’s orders.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board
item.media:credit: Joe Buglewicz for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Cumberland Fossil Plant in Tennessee is the second-largest retirement planned for 2026 after J.H. Campbell in Michigan, which has already been delayed.

item.title: Federal Chemical Safety Board Sends Warning on Trump Disaster Policy
item.description: The board said the administration is taking “a significant step backwards” on preventing chemical disasters.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: Chemical Safety and Hazard Investigation Board
item.media:credit: Marcus Constantino/Reuters
item.media:description: A chemical leak last month at Catalyst Refiners in Institute, W.Va., killed two people.

item.title: E.P.A. Moves to Weaken Water Pollution Rule for Coal Plants
item.description: The agency also said it would delay the implementation of an air pollution rule for cars and trucks by two years.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Tannen Maury/EPA, via Shutterstock
item.media:description: The Biden-era rule required coal plants to more thoroughly filter toxic metals from their wastewater before discharging it into nearby lakes, rivers and streams.

item.title: Trump’s Push to Keep Coal Plants Open Is Costing Hundreds of Millions
item.description: Costs have been mounting in the year since the Trump administration began directing aging coal plants to stay open.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.media:credit: Jim West/UCG/Universal Images Group, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The J.H. Campbell coal-fired power plant in West Olive, Mich., one of five coal plants in the United States that President Trump ordered to remain open. The plant has reported $180 million in expenses associated with running the facility since last May.

item.title: Can Some Very Tiny Particles Cool the Planet? One Tech Company Says Yes.
item.description: Stardust Solutions says its tiny spheres can reflect the sun’s rays without harming people or the environment. Critics say private companies have no business altering Earth’s atmosphere.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Avishag Shaar-Yashuv for The New York Times

item.title: A Start-Up Aiming to Make Geothermal Energy Mainstream Goes Public
item.description: Fervo Energy, which uses drilling techniques from the oil and gas industry to produce power from the earth’s heat, raised $1.9 billion in an initial public offering.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Brandon Thibodeaux for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Fervo Energy project near Milford, Utah. The company is trying to adapt drilling techniques from the oil and gas industry to unlock vast amounts of energy from the earth’s interior.

item.title: Fuel Prices Drive Sales of E.V.s, Just Not in the U.S.
item.description: Electric vehicle sales have soared in Europe and much of the rest of the world, but Americans are still hesitant.
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: High fuel prices are weighing on sales of vehicles that run on gasoline or diesel.

item.title: What to Know About the Federal Gas Tax Trump Wants to Suspend
item.description: President Trump said he would like to suspend the 18.4-cent-a-gallon tax, but it’s a move that may save drivers only a few dollars a month.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Gas prices have climbed more than 50 percent since February.

item.title: Lithuania’s Peat Bogs Could Help the Climate and Defend the Border, Too
item.description: Lithuanian officials hope restored peat bogs can reinforce the border in addition to locking away planet-warming carbon.
item.dc:creator: Avril Silva

item.title: Tom Steyer Thinks California Is Ready for a Different Climate Message
item.description: Rather than pleas to save the planet, the billionaire, a Democrat is pushing a message of affordability in his campaign to become California’s governor.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: Tom Steyer on the campaign trail last month spoke to reporters outside a gas station in Los Angeles.

item.title: Wildfires Strike Florida, Georgia and America’s ‘Wood Basket’
item.description: Large fires, fueled by a record breaking drought, have been destroying homes and timber plantations in Southeastern states.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey
item.media:credit: Joe Raedle/Getty Images
item.media:description: A firefighter from Pembroke Pines, Fla., on Monday.

item.title: Building Nests Is Hard. That’s Why Some Birds Steal.
item.description: When researchers used GPS devices to monitor the nests of Hawaiian honeycreepers, they found dozens of cases of brazen avian burglary.
item.dc:creator: Sara Novak

item.title: Oregon Prepares for a Challenging Summer of Water Shortages and High Fire Risk
item.description: After a warm winter that brought more rain than snow, the state’s snowpack was the lowest it has ever been.
item.dc:creator: Amy Graff, Anna Griffin and Loren Elliott
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Nominates Cameron Hamilton to Lead FEMA
item.description: President Trump chose Cameron Hamilton to direct federal disaster response. As acting head of FEMA last year, he had opposed abolishing the agency and was ousted.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Amanda Andrade-Rhoades for The Washington Post, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Cameron Hamilton campaigning in 2024 during his unsuccessful run as a Republican for a congressional seat in Virginia.

item.title: Trump Administration to Scrap Rule Encouraging Conservation
item.description: The Biden-era measure was intended to protect millions of acres from industrial development and the effects of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Daniel Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: The repeal of the Biden-era rule affects roughly 245 million acres of public lands, mostly in the American West, about one-tenth of the country.

item.title: As Coal Rebounds, More Toxic Mercury Is in the Air
item.description: Coal-burning power plants released more mercury last year, according to an analysis by The Times. It reverses a downward trend of emissions of a metal that interferes with brain development.
item.dc:creator: Irena Hwang and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Mike Stewart/Associated Press
item.media:description: Plant Bowen, a coal-fired power plant near Cartersville, Ga., is one of dozens of coal operations to receive presidential exemptions from meeting stricter emissions rules.

item.title: Beware of Wolves, but Straw Houses Could Help With Climate Change
item.description: Near Hudson, N.Y., a Princeton University team erected a cottage made from the agricultural byproduct, which they said is more sustainable than bricks or concrete.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times
item.media:description: A straw house built as an experiment stands 28 feet tall and has a sleeping loft, a daybed, five windows and a kitchen.

item.title: Oil Slick Is Detected Off Kharg Island in the Persian Gulf
item.description: The slick, captured in satellite images near Kharg Island in the Persian Gulf, has raised concerns about the condition of Iran’s oil infrastructure.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Mira Rojanasakul and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: via Reuters
item.media:description: An image from the European Space Agency’s Copernicus Sentinel-2 satellite showing a slick off Kharg Island on Wednesday.

item.title: David Attenborough, a Voice of Nature, Turns 100
item.description: Pictures and striking scenes from the making of perhaps the world’s most celebrated naturalist.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: John Stillwell/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Mr. Attenborough enrolled at the University of Cambridge in 1945, where he studied natural sciences, including geology and zoology.

item.title: Greenpeace Suffers Another Blow in Court Fight With Energy Transfer
item.description: In an unusual move, a North Dakota court said Greenpeace International shouldn’t be allowed to pursue a lawsuit in Europe, where it is based, against the company.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Thibaud Moritz/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Activists in France protested against the Energy Transfer lawsuit against Greenpeace USA last year.

item.title: She Tried to Help Schools Build Healthier Playgrounds. Then Her E.P.A. Grant Was Canceled.
item.description: Kirsten Beyer was assessing the benefits of improving school playgrounds in Milwaukee. Then her E.P.A. grant was canceled.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Sara Stathas for The New York Times

item.title: Wild Swimming and Stargazing in Madagascar’s Grand Canyon
item.description: A parched corner of an island often called the eighth continent rewards intrepid visitors with hidden pools, celestial wonders and extraordinary wildlife.
item.dc:creator: Meghan Davidson Ladly and Ihsaan Haffejee
item.media:credit: Ihsaan Haffejee for The New York Times

item.title: Why America Is Removing So Many ‘Deadbeat’ Dams
item.description: States are recognizing the ecological benefits of ripping out dams and reconnecting rivers.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Andrew Mangum for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Street Dam on the Susquehanna River in Harrisburg, Pa.

item.title: Trump Administration Lifts Ban on ‘Cyanide Bombs’ on Public Lands
item.description: The Bureau of Land Management will allow the use of the spring-loaded traps, which can kill coyotes and other animals that prey on livestock.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Bannock County Sheriff's Office, via Associated Press
item.media:description: An M-44 device, or “cyanide bomb,” used for predator control in Pocatello, Idaho.

item.title: Trump Panel Recommends FEMA Respond to Fewer Disasters
item.description: A White House task force called for speeding up aid and responding to fewer disasters. But some of its ideas would require action by Congress to become reality.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A FEMA staging area in Kerrville, Texas, last July, in the wake of the catastrophic flooding that destroyed an all-girls camp.

item.title: America the Undammed
item.description: More miles of the country’s rivers were reconnected last year thanks to dam removals than at any other time in history.
item.dc:creator: Cara Buckley
item.media:credit: Andrew Mangum for The New York Times
